title: What Hormuz and Chinese Sources Reveal About Beijing's Energy Strategy
gist: Despite building the world's largest energy system, China remains exposed to external shocks — over 70% of its oil is imported and its nuclear buildout increasingly depends on foreign uranium — and the Hormuz crisis exposed this vulnerability, pushing Beijing to again fall back on domestic coal as its ultimate energy-security insurance.

insights.claim: China has built the world's largest energy system and become the leading producer of electricity, the dominant manufacturer of renewable energy technologies, the world's largest EV market, and the largest consumer of oil and importer of LNG, yet it has grown more vulnerable to external energy shocks despite this scale.
insights.speaker: Joseph Dellatte
insights.so_what: China's energy dominance in production/manufacturing does not equal energy security — a key reframing for anyone modeling China's resilience to supply shocks.
insights.quote: China has become more vulnerable to external energy shocks

insights.claim: More than 70 percent of China's oil consumption depends on imports, and significant portions of its natural gas supply and its expanding nuclear sector's uranium also originate abroad.
insights.speaker: Joseph Dellatte
insights.so_what: Quantifies China's structural import dependence across oil, gas, and uranium — a concrete exposure metric for assessing China's vulnerability to a Hormuz-style chokepoint disruption or sanctions regime.
insights.quote: More than 70 percent of its oil consumption depends on imports.
insights.locator: More than 70 percent of its oil consumption

themes.theme: Hormuz crisis and the coal fallback

insights.claim: The Hormuz crisis exposed China's continued dependence on energy resources and supply chains beyond its control as a weak point in its development model.
insights.speaker: Joseph Dellatte
insights.so_what: Ties a live geopolitical flashpoint (Hormuz) directly to a specific vulnerability in China's energy security, useful for scenario-planning around Middle East chokepoint disruptions and their China spillover.
insights.quote: The Hormuz crisis has therefore exposed a weak point in China’s development model
insights.locator: The Hormuz crisis has therefore exposed

insights.claim: When confronted with external energy shocks, China ultimately falls back on its vast domestic coal resources as its ultimate energy-security insurance policy.
insights.speaker: Joseph Dellatte
insights.so_what: Reinforces the coal-demand floor thesis for China even amid its renewable and EV buildout — relevant for thermal coal and coal-miner exposure during geopolitical energy shocks.
insights.quote: China ultimately falls back on its vast domestic coal resources as its ultimate energy security insurance policy
insights.locator: It has also revealed once again
